data.title: Iren Unleashes $2B AI Bond Blitz -- But Investors Hit the Brakes
data.description:
Iren Ltd. moved into the spotlight with a financing play that could reshape how investors think about the neocloud build-out. The company is looking to raise $2 billion across two convertible bonds$1 billion due 2032 carrying a 0% to 0.25% coupon, and another $1 billion due 2033 marketed at 0.5% to 1%. People familiar with the deal said both tranches come with a 25% to 30% conversion premium, a structure that could appeal to investors betting on continued growth in AI compute demand. The timing follows Microsoft's roughly $9.7 billion, five-year deal in November to secure capacity from Iren, a signal that even smaller infrastructure players could be stepping into far larger roles as the industry races to keep up.

But the market reaction added a dose of tension. Iren's shares fell 6.4% to $45.38 in after-hours trading as of 5:13 p.m. Monday in New York, suggesting investors are still parsing the implications of such a sizable capital raise. Alongside the bond sale, the company is running a private share offering and exploring the repurchase of some of its existing convertible notes due 2029 and 2030. According to the company, part of the proceeds will go toward capped call transactions, a tool that could help manage potential dilution if the bonds convert down the road.

Citigroup , Goldman Sachs , and JPMorgan are working on the share sale and the convertible issuance, though commentary from the banks remains limited. For investors trying to gauge where Iren fits into the broader AI infrastructure race, this could be a moment that tests both sentiment and conviction. The company is leaning hard into what some view as seemingly bottomless demand for high-performance computing, and the outcome of this dual offering could be an early indicator of how much appetite remains for capital-intensive AI capacity plays.

data.title: Gold holds steady, silver scales record high
data.description:
Key points:
  • US private payrolls decrease in November, ADP says
  • Silver hits record high at $58.98 earlier in the session

By Anmol Choubey

Gold prices held steady on Wednesday, buoyed by weak private payrolls data that reinforced expectations of a U.S. interest rate cut next week, while silver hit a fresh record high.

Spot gold was little changed at $4,202.06 an ounce by 2:03 p.m. ET (1903 GMT), after hitting a session high of $4,241.29 earlier in the session.

U.S. gold futures for February delivery settled 0.3% higher at $4,232.50.

Silver steadied after touching a record high of $58.98 earlier in the session.

"This morning's miss on ADP data, combined with silver hitting all-time highs overnight," is supportive for gold, said RJO Futures senior market strategist Bob Haberkorn.

"Gold is following silver at the moment, with silver pulling back a little bit here."

U.S. private payrolls fell by 32,000 jobs in November, Wednesday's ADP employment report showed, missing economists' expectations for a 10,000-job increase.

CME's FedWatch tool now shows an 89% chance that the U.S. central bank will cut rates next week, while major brokerages also forecast an interest rate cut at the December 9-10 policy meeting.

Markets are still awaiting the delayed September Personal Consumption Expenditures data, the Fed's preferred inflation gauge, due on Friday.

Lower interest rates tend to favor non-yielding assets such as gold.

Silver is up 102% so far this year due to concerns about the market liquidity after outflows to the U.S. stocks, its inclusion in the U.S. critical minerals list and a structural supply deficit.

"The strength in silver is due to supply concerns at the exchange levels," Haberkorn said, adding that the metal could touch the $60/oz milestone shortly.

Copper prices also hit a record high on Wednesday on a weaker dollar, supply concerns and tighter availability of metal in warehouses registered with the London Metal Exchange.

Elsewhere, platinum gained 0.9% to $1,652.03 an ounce and palladium rose 0.4% to $1,466.98.

data.title: The $2.5 Billion Lithium Bet That Could Rewrite Europe's EV Future
data.description:
Investors just watched Europe fire its latest shot in the global battery-materials race, as Vulcan Energy Resources (VULNF) secured a A$3.9 billion ($2.5 billion) financing package to push its Lionheart Project from plan to construction. After months of anticipation, Vulcan says the buildout will begin within days in Germany's Upper Rhine Valley, where the company believes it is developing what could become Europe's largest lithium resource. CEO Cris Moreno pointed to a first-phase target of 24,000 tons a year, fully contracted for the next decade, and suggested that this output may support roughly half a million electric vehicles annually. The company is making a bigger claim too: geothermal heat will power the extraction, potentially giving Europe a carbon-neutral source of a material central to its energy transition.

What has captured market attention is the structure of the financing itself. Vulcan says the package blends debt, government grants, equity investments and new equity raising from partners including Hancock Prospecting, the vehicle controlled by Gina Rinehart. Germany and Australia are stepping in with as much as a combined 270 million, split between Germany's Raw Materials Fund at up to 150 million and Export Finance Australia at 120 million. Beneath the funding mechanics sits a deeper narrative: Lionheart could generate 275 gigawatt hours of power and 560 gigawatt hours of heat each year for an estimated 30 years, creating a vertically aligned energy-and-materials platform that Europe has been searching for.

Officials in Berlin and Canberra framed the project as a joint attempt to reduce future supply risks rather than merely expand production. German Economy Minister Katherina Reiche said the partnership reflects how Australia's resource base and technical capability can pair with Germany's industrial innovation to support diversified, sustainable supply chains at a moment when critical-materials access is in flux. Vulcan indicated that further equity raising involving Rinehart may still be possible, and trading in the company's shares remains halted on the ASX as markets wait for the next update.

data.title: Corn, wheat ease after gains driven by Black Sea jitters
data.description:
Key points:
  • Corn and wheat prices fall as Black Sea tensions ease
  • Ample global wheat supplies add bearish sentiment
  • Soybeans decline due to uncertainty over Chinese demand

By Julie Ingwersen

U.S. corn futures fell more than 1% on Wednesday and wheat futures also dipped as fears eased about a military escalation in the Black Sea grain export region, analysts said.

Soybeans declined on uncertainty about Chinese demand for U.S. supplies of the oilseed.

As of 1 p.m. CST (1900 GMT), Chicago Board of Trade March corn futures (CH26) were down 6-1/4 cents at $4.43-3/4 per bushel and March wheat (WH26) was down 2 cents at $5.39 a bushel. CBOT January soybeans (SF26) were down 8 cents at $11.16-3/4 a bushel.

Corn and wheat erased most of the previous day's advances, which brokers had attributed to Russian President Vladimir Putin's threat to cut off Ukraine's access to the Black Sea after drone attacks on Russia-linked vessels. By Wednesday, following a five-hour Kremlin meeting between Putin and U.S. President Donald Trump's top envoys, fears of a threat to grain exports appeared to have receded.

"They are not telling us any details, but it seems like maybe Mr. Putin is not going to try to stop Ukraine from shipping grain. He was rattling his saber yesterday," said Tom Fritz, a partner with EFG Group in Chicago.

Ample global supplies, bolstered by bumper crops being harvested in Argentina and Australia, added to bearish sentiment in wheat.

Algeria’s state grains agency bought about 810,000 to 870,000 metric tons of milling wheat in an international tender, European traders said. Algeria’s purchases are optional origin, but traders suspect some of the wheat could be sourced from the Black Sea region or Argentina.

Soybean futures declined as traders continued to monitor U.S. soy sales to China. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told the New York Times DealBook summit that China was poised to complete its commitments under a U.S.-China trade agreement, including the purchase of 12 million metric tons of soybeans, which the Treasury chief said would be finished by the end of February 2026.

"Mr. Bessent just put the whammy on the bean market," Fritz said. "He let it drop that they (China) don't have to fulfill it until the end of February. We were always under the impression that it was going to be by the end of December," Fritz said, adding that by February, Brazil will be starting to harvest what is projected to be a record-large soybean crop that could dominate global exports.

U.S. shipments to China are gaining momentum, with at least six bulk cargo vessels scheduled to load with soybeans at Gulf Coast terminals through mid-December, according to a shipping schedule seen by Reuters on Tuesday.

data.title: Basis bids mostly flat on slow farmer sales, lower futures
data.description:
Spot basis bids for hard red winter wheat held mostly steady around the U.S. Plains on Wednesday as futures prices retreated and farmer selling remained slow, grain merchants said.

  • Wheat futures on the Chicago Board of Trade fell on Wednesday for the third time in four sessions. Farmer selling has been limited as cash prices remain below recent highs.

  • The U.S. Department of Agriculture is due to release weekly export sales data early on Thursday for the week ended October 30. Analysts surveyed by Reuters expect net wheat sales between 250,000 and 650,000 metric tons, after sales of 499,778 tons the previous week.

  • K.C. March hard red winter wheat (KWH26) futures last traded 3-1/4 cents lower at $5.29-3/4 per bushel.

data.title: Marvell Stock Surges on Q3 Beat and Billion-Dollar AI Deal
data.description:
Marvell stock jumped about 5% on Wednesday morning trading after beating Q3 forecasts and unveiling a deal to buy Celestial AI for about $3.25 billion.

The deal pays $1 billion cash and about 27.2 million shares up front, with contingent earnouts tied to milestones that could raise the total to as much as $5.5 billion. Marvell expects the purchase to close in Q1 2026.

Marvell beat Q3 estimates with adjusted EPS of $0.76 versus $0.74 and revenue of $2.075 billion, up 37% year over year. Management raised data-center revenue guidance and said Celestial could expand its optical interconnect offerings starting in fiscal 2028.

Celestial builds a photonic fabric that uses light to speed data transfers inside data centers, aiming to ease bandwidth bottlenecks for large AI systems. Marvell targets a $500 million run rate by Q4 FY28 and $1 billion by Q4 FY29.

According to analysts, the acquisition has increased the AI marketability of Marvell and might reduce the number of third-party optics in the market, although the implementation risk and milestone-based earnouts (earnings) give it a one-week execution, all or nothing. The investors will be observing the outcome keenly.

data.title: Rivian Recalls 35,000 Vans Over Seat Belt Risk
data.description:
Rivian Automotive is back dealing with another recall, this time over a seat belt safety issue that's affecting thousands of vehicles across the U.S., though the stock still managed a small gain.

The company is recalling nearly 35,000 electric delivery vans after identifying a potential flaw in the driver's seat belt pretensioner cable. Federal safety officials say a damaged cable may not properly restrain a driver in a crash, raising the risk of injury. Rivian traced the problem to cases where drivers repeatedly sat on the belt instead of wearing it correctly.

Rivian is trying to reassure owners. The company has pushed out a software update over the air and says it will inspect affected vehicles and replace any damaged parts free of charge. Owners will start receiving notices by mail on Jan. 19.

The latest recall adds to a growing list this year, including a fix for hands-free driving software in September and a winter-weather headlight problem earlier in 2025.

Investors care because repeated quality issues can quietly erode confidence, even when repairs are simple, especially in a tightly competitive EV market.

data.title: Notable quotes from finance and markets speakers at the Reuters NEXT conference
data.description:
Reuters is hosting the two-day Reuters NEXT conference in New York on Wednesday and Thursday, bringing together more than 700 international business leaders and policymakers to examine the biggest issues facing society, business and the world.

The following are some notable quotes from speakers in the finance and markets sessions of the conference.

MICHAL KATZ, MIZUHO AMERICAS HEAD OF INVESTMENT AND CORPORATE BANKING

"AI is still in the early innings of its evolution, and obviously valuations have been stretched, just given the dollars going into capex and companies wanting a stake in the game. We have seen risks arise over potential overbuild, concentration among certain off-takers, and questions about whether returns will materialize. But it’s early days—the story of AI is still being written. Winners have yet to be defined."

UMESH SUBRAMANIAN, CITADEL CHIEF TECHNOLOGY OFFICER

"We try to make sure that AI doesn't go into the land of prediction. It goes into the land of seeing today clearly."

BRENDAN COUGHLIN, PRESIDENT OF CITIZENS FINANCIAL GROUP

"The industry is ripe for further consolidation and a lot of this tech discussion we've had is going to favor folks with more scale. There's been some M&A in 2025. There's a lot of discussion around that heating up. I think you know (by) December 31, 2026, there will be less deals than people imagine."

JOHN STECHER, BLACKSTONE CHIEF TECHNOLOGY OFFICER

"We are really looking at these tools as augmenting all of our great people," Stecher said, asked about applications of artificial intelligence.

Stecher said large language models were good at extracting information from documents and putting them together so "somebody can actually read it and decide, is this a deal I actually want to take a look at or not."

Those models are currently equivalent to "high-school level people" in their ability to make decisions, Stecher said, but they augment people's work.

"It's like the Iron Man suit around everybody, every man and woman that works inside the firm, it just makes them more effective."

RICK WURSTER, CHARLES SCHWAB CEO

"Our clients' wealth is at an all-time high. A lot of our clients are asking us about how to protect that wealth. Many have grown their balances beyond what they might have hoped for given what the markets have done."

"Our typical client hasn't expressed a strong need to hold the spot. They're comfortable holding the exchange-traded product and knowing that it's custody to Schwab and not in a wallet."

"M&A will be something we're going to keep our eyes out for. If we can add a capability that appeals to our clients, it's going to add enormous value to the company with the size of our asset base and client base. If we acquire a capability that appeals to them, the value in that is huge."

View the live broadcast of the World Stage here and read full coverage here.

data.title: AWS Goes All-In on Enterprise AI
data.description:
Amazon Web Services put AI front and center at its re:Invent event in Las Vegas, rolling out AI Factories, a new family of Nova models, and tools designed to make building AI faster and simpler for businesses.

CEO Matt Garman said AWS added 3.8 gigawatts of data-center capacity over the last 12 months, more than any rival, and claimed more than half of all VC-backed startups now use AWS. The biggest reveal was AI Factories, which let customers run AWS AI infrastructure inside their own data centers using Trainium chips, Nvidia GPUs, high-speed networking and Amazon's AI software.

AWS also introduced the Trainium3 chip and confirmed Trainium4 is already in development with support for Nvidia's NVLink technology. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said the partnership is helping build the compute fabric behind the AI economy.

On the software side, AWS launched Nova models, Nova Forge for custom training, and AgentCore tools for building AI agents. Reddit and Sony shared early success using the platform. Investors care because AWS isn't just selling cloud anymore, it wants to run enterprise AI end to end.

data.title: Netflix Says Bundle Would Cut Bills
data.description:
Netflix is making its case to regulators by saying a combined subscription with HBO Max would actually save viewers money, not raise prices, according to people familiar with the discussions cited by Reuters.

The company is arguing that bundling Netflix with HBO Max would cost less than paying for the two services separately. The message is aimed at easing fears that putting both platforms under one roof would reduce competition and leave customers paying more. Netflix has reportedly submitted a mostly cash bid for Warner Bros. Discovery's studio and streaming business, a deal that would dramatically expand its movie and TV lineup.

Still, the impact on Netflix's market share may be limited. Sources say most HBO Max users already pay for Netflix, meaning the main benefit for customers could be convenience and a cheaper bundle rather than entirely new content.

Investors care because Netflix is looking for ways to grow without constantly raising prices, especially as competition in streaming gets tougher.

data.title: Canadian dollar gains as oil prices rise, productivity growth improves
data.description:
Key points:
  • Canadian dollar gains 0.2% against the greenback
  • Trades in a range of 1.3939 to 1.3975
  • Price of oil increases 0.9%
  • Bond yields fall across the curve

By Fergal Smith

The Canadian dollar strengthened against its U.S. counterpart on Wednesday as oil prices rose and domestic data showed productivity rebounding in the third quarter.

The loonie was trading 0.2% higher at 1.3945 per U.S. dollar, or 71.71 U.S. cents, after trading in a range of 1.3939 to 1.3975.

Canadian labor productivity rose by 0.9% last quarter after declining 1% in the second quarter, helped by a sharp recovery in business output. It marked the sixth quarterly increase in eight quarters.

"While the rebound in productivity from the trade hit in Q2 is certainly encouraging, the big story here is the massive upward revisions to prior years," said Doug Porter, chief economist at BMO Capital Markets. "While no one is going to mistake Canada for a world leader in productivity growth, the backdrop is nowhere nearly as dismal on that front as first estimated."

Canada's jobs report for November, due on Friday, could offer additional clues on the state of the domestic economy. Economists have forecast that Canada's economy shed 5,000 jobs and that the unemployment rate edged up to 7% from 6.9% in October.

Still, the Bank of Canada has signaled that its easing campaign is on hold after the benchmark interest rate was lowered to a three-year low of 2.25% in October. Investors expect no change in rates at a policy decision next week. (0#CADIRPR)

The U.S. dollar extended recent losses against a basket of major currencies as traders raised bets on Federal Reserve rate cuts, while the price of oil was up 0.9% at $59.15 a barrel after Russia said talks with U.S. officials in Moscow failed to reach a compromise on a potential Ukraine peace deal. Oil is one of Canada's major exports.

Canadian bond yields eased across the curve, tracking moves in U.S. Treasuries. The 10-year (CA10YT=RR) was down 3 basis points at 3.221%, extending its pullback from a near three-month high at 3.277% during Tuesday's session.
